content:
9 min read
Just now

--

Closing the series with a look back — the parts I’d argue with, the costs I skipped, and where the framework holds up, and where it doesn’t.
I built this series in a rush of genuine excitement, and excitement inflates things — the numbers sound harder, the rules sound more permanent, the naming grander than it earned. The ideas underneath are sound. This piece is me separating the two.
Every piece in this series so far has been a field report: here is what we did, here is why it worked, here is the oracle that caught the thing we would otherwise have shipped. This is the last of the broad, framework-level pieces before I move on to narrower, hands-on work. And read as a whole, it has a blind spot: the series reads like a finished standard. It isn’t one, and I never meant it to be. It’s a running account of a team figuring this out in public, written to start a conversation, not to end one.
So the most useful thing I can do next is not another win. It’s to turn the same skepticism the whole series preaches — never trust the agent, trust the oracle — back on the series itself. I argued in an earlier piece that the reviewer can never be the author; this is the one review I can’t hand off, so read it as exactly that — an author grading his own work, with all the blind spots that implies. If the argument is any good, it should survive me attacking it. If it isn’t, better that I find the cracks than that you inherit them.
None of what follows is a retraction — the method holds; I’m here to calibrate it, not recant it. Here is where I think this breaks.
1. The evidence is thinner than it sounds
The marketplace piece leads with a number: work we had estimated at roughly 37 engineering days — about nine calendar weeks at a normal cadence — came in at 11 calendar days, which we called a roughly 4x beat against the estimate. I stand by the direction of that. I do not want anyone treating the magnitude as data.
That 37-day figure was our own pre-AI estimate — no control group, no second team building the same thing the old way. We are all still learning how much AI actually moves the needle, and while the discipline in this series clearly helped, it can’t claim to be the only reason for the gains. The honest reading: we beat our own estimate by a lot, and our own estimate is exactly the kind of number to hold loosely.
Every result in this series has the same shape. It’s one team, one stack, self-reported, with the successes written up by the people who designed the process. That doesn’t make it worthless — a detailed field report from real production work is more useful than most of what’s published on this topic — but it is evidence of existence (“this can work”), not evidence of magnitude (“this is 4x better”). Read it as the former.
2. I left the cost off the scorecard
Go back through the series and count what the workflow actually spends. A read-only exploration pass. A hardened spec. Implementation in small compilable steps, each stopped for human diff review. A separate character-set check. A code review in a fresh session that didn’t write the code. Sometimes three rounds of that review. Characterization harnesses stood up before a refactor can even begin. A three-way merge simulation before every reconcile.
I described all of that as if it were free. It is not. It is a real tax in human hours and in tokens, and I never subtracted it from the speedup I was celebrating. The honest scorecard has to net the two — but against the right baseline. The comparison was never ceremony versus raw typing speed; it’s ceremony versus the cost of the mess it prevents — the silent regression, the debt, the incident you clean up three weeks later. And that’s the trap in the numbers: the ceremony is visible and paid up front, while the mess is hidden and paid later, so the process always looks more expensive than it is. Netted that way it still loses for anything small — which is why the guardrail piece has a hotfix escape hatch — and it wins the moment the deferred cost of drift would have swamped the up-front cost of discipline. I never named that threshold. I should have, because “when is this worth it” is a better question than “how fast was it once.”
The honest scorecard has to net the two — but against the right baseline. The comparison was never ceremony versus raw typing speed; it’s ceremony versus the cost of the mess it prevents — the silent regression, the debt, the incident you clean up three weeks later.
And that’s the trap in the numbers: the ceremony is visible and paid up front, while the mess is hidden and paid later, so the process always looks more expensive than it is.
Netted that way it still loses for anything small — which is why the guardrail piece has a hotfix escape hatch — and it wins the moment the deferred cost of drift would have swamped the up-front cost of discipline. I never named that threshold. I should have, because “when is this worth it” is a better question than “how fast was it once.”
3. I mixed timeless principle with dated scar tissue
Some of what this series presents as method is genuine principle — match the oracle to the failure mode, the reviewer can’t be the author, a doneness claim is not evidence — and it should still hold in five years.
But a lot of it is scar tissue from mid-2026 tooling wearing the same authoritative voice: the ASCII-only linter exists because a shell cp once padded a file with NUL bytes, and "the human runs every command" is half principle, half workaround for agents that patch around red bars when you let them loose.
Filing all of that under “the blueprint” was a mistake — it dates the framework and invites cargo-culting a guard for a bug that’ll be patched in a release or two. The honest version: the zero-autonomy stance is contingent, right for the agents I have now, and a framework worth keeping should say out loud which of its rules are permanent and which are temporary. This one didn’t.
4. The ceremony has a gravity toward bureaucracy
The AGENTS.md filesystem looks like a lot, and taken as a flat list it is. But the weight is scoped to the work: the large track exists for genuinely large, multi-phase efforts, while day-to-day changes ride the small track or the hotfix hatch.
And on a team it distributes rather than multiplies — an architect owns a workstream, members take phases, a couple run in parallel. It was never ten people each spinning up ten workstreams on one system.
What I under-explained in the series is that this is machinery for large designs, not overhead for every edit. The open question I’ll still own: whether that discipline holds for people who didn’t build it, under a deadline.
5. It’s more of a typed-monorepo story than I admitted
“The compiler is often a better net than tests” is one of the load-bearing claims in the legacy-refactor piece. It is also only really true in a strict, strongly typed monorepo — which is exactly what we have. A mis-scoped edit doesn’t ship there; it fails the build. That’s a genuinely powerful oracle, and much of the safety story quietly leans on it being nearly omniscient.
Move to a Python service, a loosely typed JS codebase, or anything where the type-checker sees a fraction of the surface, and that rung of the ladder gets a lot weaker. The characterization-test approach is my honest answer for behavior the types can’t reach, and it partly covers this — but the overall confidence of the series doesn’t degrade the way it should when you leave typed-monorepo territory.
If you’re in a dynamic-language shop, treat the compiler rung as mostly unavailable to you and lean much harder on characterization tests and review. The series should have said that plainly instead of generalizing from the environment where it’s strongest.
6. The branding oversells the novelty
Strip the name off it and much of “Agentic Engineering” is just good software engineering — spec first, small steps, review, a human in the loop — aimed at a new kind of collaborator. That aim is useful.
But the packaging (“Beyond Vibe Coding,” “the manifesto,” “the blueprint”) implies a new paradigm, and most of what’s here is old rigor reasserted, not something I invented.
Two things I do think are new, and I’ll defend them: matching a specific oracle to a specific failure mode, and the volatile-branch reconnaissance method — buckets not diffs, recompute the merge-base every run, predict-then-verify. The rest is discipline the field already had; I just pointed it somewhere new.
If anything earns the eye-roll it’s the naming, and I’d sooner drop the grand labels than defend them.
The order I’d keep exactly as it was — the experience and the why first, because that’s where the work started, then the walkthroughs as proof. The overreach was in what I called it, not in how it was built.
7. The hardest thing I claimed hasn’t actually landed
The work I’m most confident about — the marketplace API — is also the most conventional: multi-service, but a fairly normal build-and-review cycle. The work that would most impress you if it succeeds — the months-long engine rebuild on a 791-file volatile branch, the god-facade retirement still referenced in 200-plus files, the shape-unification with the expensive clone still called in a dozen places — is still in flight.
I flagged that in the pieces, which I’m glad about. But I want to say it more plainly here: the strongest evidence and the strongest claims in this series do not yet fully overlap. The volatile-branch method has carried the branch through eight reconciles and counting — it’s reconciled weekly, on purpose, because that cadence is the process, not a one-off stunt for a few commits. That’s exactly why it won’t rot. But “reconciled weekly and healthy” still isn’t “landed and shipped,” and I won’t call the method fully proven until the rebuild lands. I’ll report that outcome when it comes.
What I still believe
None of this retracts the spine. The core reframe — the model is a fast, context-blind collaborator, so you surround it with oracles that fail loudly instead of trusting its output — I believe more, not less, after arguing with myself. The specific mechanics that flow from it (a reviewer who didn’t write the code, a spec you check against instead of a vibe, an oracle matched to the failure mode) keep earning their keep. The critiques above are about calibration: how confident I was, what I left out of the accounting, and how much I let a snapshot of today’s tooling pose as permanent truth.
That’s the distinction I care about most: everything above is a calibration, not a retraction. I am confident the method holds — the series was meant to start a conversation about solving new problems in new ways. I was just too confident about how much, too quiet about the cost, and too willing to let today’s solutions and ideas sound permanent. That’s a correction worth making in public, and it’s the right note to end the framework on. From here, the writing gets narrower: specific tools, specific problems.
The takeaway
If you take one thing from this piece, take the frame I should have led the whole series with: this is a field report from inside one team’s ongoing experiment, not a standard to comply with. The metrics are directional, the ceremony is negotiable, half the workarounds have a shelf life, and the biggest bet is still open on the table.
So treat the series the way the series tells you to treat an agent’s output: don’t trust it because it’s confident and well-formatted. Trust the parts that survive an oracle you run yourself — your codebase, your stack, your team, your deadline. Tell me where it broke for you. That’s the conversation I was actually trying to start.
Further reading
The full series, if you want to see what I’m arguing with:

• Beyond Vibe Coding: Embracing Agentic Engineering for Sustainable AI Development

• Beyond Vibe Coding: Using AGENTS.md to Build a File System That Enforces Code Discipline on AI

• Beyond Vibe Coding
